getting-started-install-burrito--docs.name: Docs
getting-started-install-burrito--docs.title: Getting Started/Install Burrito
getting-started-install-burrito--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' # Install Burrito This document will guide you through the steps required to get Burrito working in your React application. ## Install the dependency First you will need to install the Burrito NPM dependency. You can do so by running the following command: ```bash # npm npm i @janeapp/burrito-design-system # pnpm pnpm add @janeapp/burrito-design-system # yarn yarn add @janeapp/burrito-design-system ``` ## Set it up with your bundler Burrito is shipped with compiled CSS files that are loaded in the JS bundle. You will need to ensure that your bundler can handle this. We documented the solutions we found for a few bundlers. If your use case is not documented and you found how to make it work, make sure to share it with FF so we can add it here. If you're having trouble configuring your bundler, make sure to reach out to FF for assistance. ### Using Vite #### Using Burrito with Vanilla Extract Install the [@vanilla-extract/vite-plugin](https://vanilla-extract.style/documentation/integrations/vite/) plugin and use it in your Vite config: ```diff plugins: [ /* Other plugins */ + vanillaExtractPlugin({ esbuildOptions: { loader: { '.css': 'empty' } } }), ], ``` ## Using Burrito with other styling technologies By default, the `tokens` and `vars` objects are available through the package’s main entry point. ```tsx import { tokens, vars } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' ``` However, if you’re using a different styling system (for example, Tailwind) and need these objects in your configuration, you can access them through a separate export. ```tsx import { tokens, vars } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/tokens' ``` [Learn more about using the design tokens](?path=/docs/foundations-design-tokens--docs#design-tokens) ## Import the global theme To enable Burrito styling in your app you will need to import the `globalTheme` stylesheet as a side effect at the root of your application. This provides the CSS variables that Burrito uses to style components. **Note for Module Federation:** This will not work when using Module Federation, you will need to wrap your module in a [BurritoProvider](?path=/docs/components-burritoprovider--docs) ``` import '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/globalTheme' ``` ## Configure JSX A11Y for ESLint Burrito exports a JSX A11Y configuration that maps our components to their semantic HTML elements for accessibility linting with [eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y](https://github.com/jsx-eslint/eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y). This helps ensure proper accessibility rules are applied when using Burrito components.
📣 Note: This configuration is designed for ESLint 9 with flat config format.
Import the configuration from the dedicated `/eslint-jsx-a11y-config` export path: ```javascript import { jsxA11yConfig } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/eslint-jsx-a11y-config' import jsxA11y from 'eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y' export default [ // ... other config { plugins: { 'jsx-a11y': jsxA11y, }, settings: { 'jsx-a11y': jsxA11yConfig, }, rules: { ...jsxA11y.configs.recommended.rules, }, }, ] ``` ## Importing the font files Burrito, and Jane at large, uses Proxima Nova as the default typeface. Unless you're working on the monolith, you will need to make the font files available in your SPA. There is a large variety of bundlers, tech stack and CDN options these days that it is impossible to have a single way of exposing static assets from an NPM package into an SPA. To offer the maximum flexibility, Burrito ships the font files in different ways. **Note:** In many cases you will need to configure your bundler to handle static assets. ### Static import with CSS The easiest way to get the font files from Burrito is to import the `fonts.css` file at the root of your project. ```tsx // main.tsx import React from 'react' import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client' import App from './App.tsx' import './index.css' import '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/fonts.css' ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render() ``` ### ES module import If you want to include the font files through your Javascript code you can use this import: ```tsx import { injectGlobal } from 'styled-components' import * as fonts from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/fonts' injectGlobal` @font-face { font-family: 'Proxima Nova'; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; src: local('Proxima Nova'), url('${fonts.proximanovaJaneSymbolsWebfontWoff2}') format('woff2'), url('${fonts.proximanovaJaneSymbolsWebfontWoff2}') format('woff'); } @font-face { font-family: 'Proxima Nova'; font-weight: 400; font-style: italic; src: local('Proxima Nova'), url('${fonts.proximanovaRegularItalicJanesymbolsWebfontWoff2}') format('woff2'), url('${fonts.proximanovaRegularItalicJanesymbolsWebfontWoff}') format('woff'); } @font-face { font-family: 'Proxima Nova'; font-weight: 600; font-style: normal; src: local('Proxima Nova'), url('${fonts.proximanovaSboldWebfontWoff2}') format('woff2'), url('${fonts.proximanovaSboldWebfontWoff}') format('woff'); } @font-face { font-family: 'Proxima Nova'; font-weight: 600; font-style: italic; src: local('Proxima Nova'), url('${fonts.proximanovaSboldItalicWebfontWoff2}') format('woff2'), url('${fonts.proximanovaSboldItalicWebfontWoff}') format('woff'); } ` ``` ### Manually using the static files If none of the above works with your tech stack and you need to manually inject the font files in your static assets (for example a `public/` folder), you may want to manually copy and paste the files in your repository. If that's the case, you can grab them directly from the [Burrito repository](https://github.com/janeapp/burrito-design-system/tree/main/src/assets/fonts).

getting-started-figma-for-developers--do.name: Docs
getting-started-figma-for-developers--do.title: Getting Started/Figma for developers
getting-started-figma-for-developers--do.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'

<Meta title="Getting Started/Figma for developers" />

# Figma for developers

This page contains tips & tricks to help developers make the most of Figma designs using Burrito components

## Component properties

Similarly to the React component library that is available to you, there is a Figma kit that designers use
to create their designs with Burrito. As a developer, you can use Figma to know what component to use as
well as which prop to use with that component.

Select the component in the Figma design to show details in the "Properties" bar on the right side.

![A screenshot of Figma showing a selected Button and its properties in the "Properties" sidebar](./figma-for-developers/inspect-component.png)

In the example above we can see at the top that the [Button](?path=/docs/components-button--docs) component
is being used.
There is a large text area that contains a description about the component as well as links to
the design and the dev documentation about the component (The "View in Figma" and "View documentation") will
also take you there.
The "Component properties" section will give details about how this component is used to achieve the effect
you see. The Burrito team tries to maintain parity between Figma properties and React props but due to their
inherently different natures it's not always possible. The Storybook documentation contains information
and code examples you can use to achieve the same result.

### Nested components

The "Properties bar" will also show the properties of any Burrito component nested under the one you've selected.

![A screenshot of Figma showing a selected nested component and its properties in the "Properties" sidebar](./figma-for-developers/inspect-nested-component.png)

## Using design tokens

Instead of hardcoding values, your designer will use Burrito design tokens from the Figma kit to complete their designs.
The "Properties" tab will indicate what tokens are used. The token referenced in Figma are documented in Storybook on
the [Design Tokens](?path=/docs/foundations-design-tokens--docs) page

![A screenshot of Figma showing two selected buttons and their properties in the "Properties" sidebar](./figma-for-developers/inspect-layout-properties.png)

foundations-design-tokens--docs.name: Docs
foundations-design-tokens--docs.title: Foundations/Design Tokens
foundations-design-tokens--docs.content:
import { Meta, Source } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { tokens } from './index'

<Meta title="Foundations/Design Tokens" />

# Design tokens

The `tokens` object is divided in two distincts namespaces: `global` and `alias`.
The `alias` tokens reference the `global` tokens to set their own values, are
given more semantic names, and we generally recommend you reach for them first.

You can learn more about the tokens in each namespace in their respective
stories:

- [Global tokens](?path=/docs/foundations-global-tokens--docs)
- [Alias tokens](?path=/docs/foundations-alias-tokens--docs)

Jump to a reference of all the [Burrito design tokens](#design-token-reference).

## Using the tokens

Burrito utilizes [Vanilla Extract](https://vanilla-extract.style/), a
pre-processed CSS-in-JS library. Unlike runtime CSS-in-JS libraries
(styled-components, Emotion, etc.), Vanilla Extract compiles the styling with
the bundler and uses **CSS variables** to distribute the token values.

To leverage the tokens you can use the set of CSS variables generated
by Vanilla Extract and provided in a `.css.ts` file.

```ts
// style.css.ts
import { style } from '@vanilla-extract/css'
import { vars } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'

export const containerStyle = style({
  padding: vars.global.size['2x'],
  borderWidth: vars.alias.border.width.sm,
  borderColor: vars.alias.border.color.default,
  borderRadius: vars.alias.border.radius.md,
  // ...
})
```

### Using the raw token values

If you need to use the raw CSS values of the design tokens, you can import the
`tokens` JSON object directly:

```tsx
import { tokens } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/tokens'
```

## Overriding the tokens to create a custom theme

You can use the [BurritoProvider](?path=/docs/components-burritoprovider--docs)
component to apply custom branding.

## Design token reference

You can find below the raw design token values for the default Burrito theme.

<Source code={JSON.stringify(tokens, null, '  ')} language="json" />.

foundations-global-tokens--docs.name: Docs
foundations-global-tokens--docs.title: Foundations/Global Tokens
foundations-global-tokens--docs.content:
import { Meta, Source, Canvas } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'

import { color, colorPairing } from './color'
import { shadow } from './shadow'
import { size } from './size'
import { typography } from './typography'

import * as ColorStories from './color.stories'
import * as ShadowStories from './shadow.stories'
import * as SizeStories from './size.stories'

<Meta title="Foundations/Global Tokens" />

# Global Tokens

Global tokens are the foundational design attributes that apply universally across a product or suite of products.
They are "global" because they are not tied to specific components but rather apply broadly to ensure design consistency
across different contexts. Global tokens usually point to raw, static values, such as a hex code for colour, or pixel
values for spacing.

## Color & color pairing

The color tokens come with a symmetrical set of color pairing tokens. The color pairing tokens are used to match the best text color (black or white) with a given background color.
The story below demonstrates the use of `color` tokens for background colors and `colorPairing` tokens for text colors.

<Canvas of={ColorStories.Colors} sourceState="none" />

### Default token values

#### Color

<Source code={JSON.stringify(color, null, '  ')} language="json" />

#### Color pairing

<Source code={JSON.stringify(colorPairing, null, '  ')} language="json" />

## Shadow

<Canvas of={ShadowStories.Shadows} sourceState="none" />

### Default token values

<Source code={JSON.stringify(shadow, null, '  ')} language="json" />

## Size

The size token offers the various sizes of the Burrito Design System.

<Canvas of={SizeStories.Size} sourceState="none" />

### Default token values

<Source code={JSON.stringify(size, null, '  ')} language="json" />

## Typography

We recommend you use the typography components [Text](http://localhost:6006/?path=/docs/components-typography-text--docs)
and [Heading](?path=/docs/components-typography-heading--docs) to leverage the typography tokens.

### Default token value

foundations-alias-tokens--docs.content:
import { Meta, Source, Canvas } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'

import { background } from './background'
import { border } from './border'
import { icon } from './icon'
import { text } from './text'

import * as BackgroundStories from './background.stories'
import * as BorderStories from './border.stories'
import * as TextStories from './text.stories'

<Meta title="Foundations/Alias Tokens" />

# Alias Tokens

Alias tokens are tokens that reference other tokens (often global) instead of referencing a hard-coded value. Alias
tokens are used as a second layer, to add semantic meaning and help manage tokens more effectively, especially as
systems scale and evolve.

## Background

### Background color

<Canvas of={BackgroundStories.Color} sourceState="none" />

### Default token values

<Source code={JSON.stringify(background, null, '  ')} language="json" />

foundations-mixins--docs.name: Docs
foundations-mixins--docs.title: Foundations/Mixins
foundations-mixins--docs.content:
import { Meta, Source, Canvas, Title } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' import * as ButtonStories from '../../components/Button/Button.stories' import * as TextStories from '../../components/Text/Text.stories' import * as ShadowStories from '../tokens/shadow.stories' Mixins Burrito offers a collection of mixin functions that can be used to apply styles to elements. Generally, we advise reaching for the provided components first to accomplish a given task. However, if you find yourself needing to apply a style to an element that isn't covered by a component, these functions can be helpful to adhere to our design system without needing to reach for individual design tokens.
📣 Note: Mixins are specifically written internally for `vanilla-extract` and are not designed for use outside of Burrito.
**Available Mixins:** - [`mixins.bodyText`](#mixinsbodytext) - [`mixins.focus`](#mixinsfocus) - [`mixins.shadow`](#mixinsshadow) - [`mixins.truncate`](#mixinstruncate) - [`mixins.visuallyHidden`](#mixinsvisuallyhidden) ## `mixins.bodyText` Used to apply body text styles to an element. ### Usage **Use in `vanilla-extract` styles:** ```ts // style.css.ts import { recipe } from '@vanilla-extract/recipes' import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const textStyle = recipe({ base: { // Apply 'lg' body text styles ...mixins.bodyText('lg'), }, // ... }) ``` **Use in JSX:** ```tsx // Text.tsx import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const ParagraphText = () => { return

This is a paragraph of text.

} ``` ### Arguments - `size`: The desired text size. - `sm`: Small text - `md`: Medium text (default) - `lg`: Large text ### Example ## `mixins.focus` Used to apply focus styles to an element. ### Usage **Use in `vanilla-extract` styles:** ```ts // style.css.ts import { recipe } from '@vanilla-extract/recipes' import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const buttonStyle = recipe({ base: { ...mixins.focus(), }, // ... }) ``` **Use in JSX:** ```tsx // Button.tsx import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const Button = ({ children, onClick }) => ( ) ``` ## `mixins.shadow` Used to apply shadow styles to an element. Users can optionally specify the desired shadow level and direction. ### Usage **Use in `vanilla-extract` styles:** ```ts // style.css.ts import { recipe } from '@vanilla-extract/recipes' import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const containerStyle = recipe({ base: { // Apply a shadow of level 'lg' and direction 'b' ...mixins.shadow('lg', 'bottom'), }, // ... }) ``` **Use in JSX:** ```tsx // Card.tsx import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const Card = ({ children }) =>
{children}
``` ### Arguments - `level`: The desired shadow level. - `sm`: Small shadow - `md`: Medium shadow (default) - `lg`: Large shadow - `xl`: Extra large shadow - `direction`: The desired shadow direction. - `top`: Top shadow - `bottom`: Bottom shadow (default) - `left`: Left shadow - `right`: Right shadow ### Example ## `mixins.truncate` Used to apply text truncation styles to an element ## Usage **Use in `vanilla-extract` styles:** ```ts // style.css.ts import { recipe } from '@vanilla-extract/recipes' import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const textStyle = recipe({ base: { ...mixins.truncateText(), }, // ... }) ``` **Use in JSX:** ```tsx // Text.ts import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const ParagraphText = () => { return

This is a paragraph of text.

} ``` ## Example _Example of a truncated text used in the button component_ ## `mixins.visuallyHidden` Used to apply styles to visually hide an element while keeping it accessible to screen readers. ### Usage **Use in `vanilla-extract` styles:** ```ts // style.css.ts import { recipe } from '@vanilla-extract/recipes' import { mixins } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system' export const textStyle = recipe({ base: { ...mixins.visuallyHidden(), }, // ... }) ``` ## Use Burrito `mixins` with `styled-components` To see how to use it, check these [examples in the Jane repository](https://github.com/janeapp/jane/blob/master/app/assets/javascripts/admin/views/components/community_kitchen/StyledMixinComponent/StyledMixinComponent.tsx) and to explore a live example, visit this [Jane Storybook page](https://storybook.jane.qa/?path=/docs/community-kitchen-styled-components-with-mixins--docs) For more details on why Burrito `mixins` needs to be converted to kebab-case to use with `styled-components`, you can refer to [this explanation](https://github.com/janeapp/jane/blob/master/app/assets/javascripts/admin/views/components/community_kitchen/Gadgets/README.md#using-burrito-mixins-with-styled-components). If you're using `styled-components` with Burrito `mixins` inside the **Jane repository**, there's already a utility function called [convertToKebabCase](https://github.com/janeapp/jane/blob/master/app/assets/javascripts/admin/views/components/community_kitchen/Gadgets/camelcase-to-kebabcase.ts) available in the repo. For more information, check out the [utility README](https://github.com/janeapp/jane/blob/master/app/assets/javascripts/admin/views/components/community_kitchen/Gadgets/README.md#camelcasetokebabcase-utility).

components-buttons-iconbutton--docs.name: Docs
components-buttons-iconbutton--docs.title: Components/Buttons/IconButton
components-buttons-iconbutton--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as IconButtonStories from './IconButton.stories.tsx'

<Meta title="Components/Buttons/IconButton" />

<Title> Icon Button </Title>

**Icon Buttons** are buttons without a visual text label that can be used when space is limited.

<Canvas of={IconButtonStories.Default} />
<Controls of={IconButtonStories.Default} />

## Usage

### Default Usage

<Canvas
  of={IconButtonStories.Default}
  sourceState="shown"
  source={{
    code: `
import { IconButton } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'
import { Cog } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/icons'\n
export const App = () => {
  return (
    <IconButton aria-label="Settings button" onClick={() => {}}>
      <Cog aria-hidden />
    </IconButton>
  )
}`,
  }}
/>

### Kinds

Developers can choose from four types of icon buttons: `IconButton`, `IconCriticalButton`, `IconPlainButton`, and `IconBasicButton`.

<Canvas of={IconButtonStories.Kinds} />

### Sizes

The `<IconButton>` component comes in three sizes: `small`, `medium`, and `large`. You can change it with the size prop and its default size is `medium`.

<Canvas of={IconButtonStories.Sizes} />

### IconButton with Tooltip

You should typically use a Tooltip to explain the purpose of an Icon Button.

<Canvas 
  of={IconButtonStories.IconButtonWithTooltip}
  source={{
    code: `
import { IconButton, Tooltip, useTooltip } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'
import { Cog } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system/icons'\n
export const App = () => {
    const { triggerProps, tooltipProps } = useTooltip({ defaultIsOpen: true })

    return (
      <>
        <IconButton {...args} aria-label="Icon Button" {...triggerProps}>
          <Cog aria-hidden />
        </IconButton>
        <Tooltip {...tooltipProps}>Tooltips provide helpful context</Tooltip>
      </>
    )

}`,
}}
/>

## Accessibility

### Ensure the IconButton is labelled

The `IconButton` component needs to have an accessible aria-label or aria-labelledby.

### Set the type property for IconButton

By default, IconButton sets its type property to 'button', which handles most use cases. Set type='submit' explicitly when needed

### Accessibility test Status

#### General tests

| Test                                                                              | WCAG Level                                                                                             | Status                              |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| Contrast between the IconButton content and a white background is at least 4:5:1. | **AA** ([1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" /> |

#### Keyboard navigation tests

| Test                                                                                  | WCAG Level                                                                                             | Status                             |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- |
| IconButtons do not trap focus during keyboard navigation.                             | **A** ([2.1.2 Keyboard trap](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/no-keyboard-trap.html))       | <A11yTestStatus status="success"/> |
| IconButtons display clearly visible focus indicators during keyboard navigation.      | **AA** ([2.4.7 Focus Visible](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/focus-visible.html))         | <A11yTestStatus status="success"/> |
| The IconButton keyboard focus indicator has conformant size, perimeter, and contrast. | **AAA** ([2.4.13 Focus appearance](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/focus-appearance.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success"/> |

#### Screen reader tests

| Test                                                                                                         | WCAG Level                                                                                           | Status                                 |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| The IconButton is announced by screen readers with the role “button” during keyboard navigation              | **A** ([4.1.2 Name, role, value](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/name-role-value.html))  | <A11yTestStatus status="success"/>     |
| IconButtons are announced by screen readers in the order they appear on the page during keyboard navigation. | **A** ([1.3.2 Meaningful sequence](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/meaningful-sequence)) | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional"/> |

## API

### Props

In addition to the default `<IconButton>` component, there is also
`<IconBasicButton>`, `<IconCriticalButton>`, and `<IconPlainButton>`. Each of them has all
the props present on a [HTMLButtonElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLButtonElement)
plus the following:

| Prop               | Type      | required | default | Description                                                                                               |
| ------------------ | --------- | -------- | ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **`size`**         | `string`  | No       | `md`    | The size of the IconButton, which can be either `sm` (small), `md` (medium), or `lg` (large).             |
| **`isLoading`**    | `boolean` | No       | `false` | Whether the IconButton is in a "loading" or "busy" state while a command is executing.                    |
| **`isMenuButton`** | `boolean` | `false`  | `false` | Whether the Button should be considered a `Menu` button. This will rotate the icon when the Menu is open. |

For accessibility compliance, the `IconButton` component requires either the `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` property. If you omit both, the component will throw an exception.

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=17123-3194">
  Icon Button component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

components-form-inputs-datepicker--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as TextInputStories from '../TextInput/TextInput.stories.tsx'

<Meta title="Components/Form Inputs/DatePicker" />

<Title>Date Picker</Title>

While a Burrito `DatePicker` component is not yet available, it is recommended
to simply apply the `type='date'` or `type='datetime-local'` attribute to a
[**Text Input**](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-textinput--docs) for
accepting date input.

<Canvas of={TextInputStories.DatePicker} />

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=7583-754">
  Date Picker component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

components-form-inputs-fieldset--docs.name: Docs
components-form-inputs-fieldset--docs.title: Components/Form Inputs/Fieldset
components-form-inputs-fieldset--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' import { Design } from '@storybook/addon-designs/blocks' import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components' import * as FieldsetStories from './Fieldset.stories.tsx' Fieldset **Fieldsets** include the set of components needed to facilitate and describe user input for multiple input controls. They provide layout, a legend, helper text, and an error message for the group of input fields they contain. See the related [**FormField** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-formfield--docs) for use with fields that only require a single input control like **TextInputs** and **TextAreas**. ## Usage ### Basic The `
` component is used to wrap a set of form input controls, like a group of `` or a ``. A visible label is normally required for a group of form inputs, and so a `` should almost always be included as a descendent of the `
`. Optionally, a `` can be included _above_ the form inputs, and, when applicable, a `` can be included _below_ the form inputs to describe a validation error. Each input control should also be wrapped with a `` which will ensure consistent spacing and connect the optional `` that describes a specific input control (like a single checkbox item). Burrito form inputs will share their state and attributes through the `Fieldset` and `FormField` Contexts, so you can primarly apply logic to the form input components themselves. Custom `id` attributes can be applied to any of these components, including the form inputs, without any expected issues. { return (
Group legend Helper text should be as short as possible. Checkbox A Checkbox B Sometimes a checkbox may require its own contextual helper text. A clear, concise, and helpful error message.
) } ` }}/> ### Checkbox combinations ### Radio combinations ## Accessibility ### Legend input field groups should have an associated visible label. Since each input will already have its own `

components-form-inputs-formfield--docs.title: Components/Form Inputs/FormField
components-form-inputs-formfield--docs.content: import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' import { Design } from '@storybook/addon-designs/blocks' import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components' import * as FormFieldStories from './FormField.stories.tsx' Form Field **Form Fields** include the set of components needed to facilitate and describe user input for a single input control. They provide layout, a label, helper text, and an error message for the input field they contain. See the related [**Fieldset** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-fieldset--docs) for use with fields requiring multiple input controls, namely **Checkboxes** and **Radios**. ## Usage The `` component is used to wrap a form input component, like a `` or a `

components-form-inputs-numberinput--docs.name: Docs
components-form-inputs-numberinput--docs.title: Components/Form Inputs/NumberInput
components-form-inputs-numberinput--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' import * as NumberInputStories from './NumberInput.stories.tsx' Number Input **Number Inputs** are form fields that allow a user to write or edit a single line numeric value. See the related [**Form Field** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-formfield--docs) for augmenting the Number Input with a label, helper text and a validation error message. ## Usage ) => { setValue(event.target.value) } return ` }}/> ### Value Note that the `value` is still of type `string` and will need to be explicitly converted to a `number` if that is required (for example with [parseInt()](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/parseInt)). To prevent the submission of non-numeric input, you will need to ensure that appropriate form validation is applied with this component.
Accessibility note:
Normally a NumberInput will be used with a label element to provide a visible label for the field, instead of an aria-label attribute that will only be available to assistive technologies (like screen readers).
## API ### Props All props and behaviour for this component are the same as the Burrito [**Text Input**](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-textinput--docs), except that the `type` attribute is not allowed and the `inputmode` attribute has a default value of `decimal`.

components-form-inputs-searchinput--docs.name: Docs
components-form-inputs-searchinput--docs.title: Components/Form Inputs/SearchInput
components-form-inputs-searchinput--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as SearchInputStories from './SearchInput.stories.tsx'

<Meta title="Components/Form Inputs/SearchInput" />

<Title>Search Input</Title>

**Search Inputs** are form fields that allow a user to enter search queries.

<Canvas of={SearchInputStories.Default} />
<Controls of={SearchInputStories.Default} />

## Usage

The `<SearchInput>` can generally be used just like a Burrito
[**Text Input**](/docs/components-form-inputs-textinput--docs),
but for the specific purpose of searching.

### Default Usage

<Canvas
  of={SearchInputStories.Default}
  sourceState="shown"
  source={{
    code: `
import { SearchInput } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'\n
const MyApp = () => {
  const [value, setValue] = React.useState('')
  const handleChange = (event: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) => {
    setValue(event.target.value)
  }
  return <SearchInput aria-label="search" value={value} onChange={handleChange} />
}`,
  }}
/>

### With Search Landmark

The **Search Input** component can be used with a
[search landmark](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Reference/Roles/search_role)
to indicate that the input is for searching. This is useful for screen readers
and other assistive technologies. When more than one search landmark is present
on a page, an `aria-label` should be used to indicate the purpose.

Wrapping the **Search Input** in a `<form>` ensures that if the user presses enter,
the search handler will be called.

<Canvas of={SearchInputStories.WithLandmark} />

## Accessibility

### Hiding label text

Normally, form inputs must have a visible label to help users understand what
information they need to provide. **Search inputs** are a notable exception—when
they are placed next to a button labeled “Search” then the purpose is clear even
if the label itself isn't visible.

For users that rely on assistive technology, an `aria-label` attribute must
still be used to provide a label.

### Accessibility test status

The **Search Input** component has also been tested for accessibility criteria that
applies to the Burrito
[**Text Input** component](/docs/components-form-inputs-textinput--docs).

### General tests

| Test                                                               | WCAG Level                                                                                              | Status                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Search Input icon meets color contrast requirement of at least 3:1 | **AA** ([1.4.11 Non-text Contrast](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/non-text-contrast.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" /> |

### Screen reader tests

| Test                                                              | WCAG Level                                                                                                      | Status                                  |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Screen reader announces meaningful label upon initial interaction | **A** ([3.3.2 Labels or Instructions](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/labels-or-instructions.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" /> |

## API

### Props

All props and behaviour for the `<SearchInput>` are the same as the Burrito
[**Text Input**](/docs/components-form-inputs-textinput--docs),
except that the `type` attribute is not allowed since it is set to `search`.

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=7583-325">
  Search Input component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

components-form-inputs-timepicker--docs.title: Components/Form Inputs/TimePicker
components-form-inputs-timepicker--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as TextInputStories from '../TextInput/TextInput.stories.tsx'

<Meta title="Components/Form Inputs/TimePicker" />

<Title>Time Picker</Title>

While a Burrito `TimePicker` component is not yet available, it is recommended
to simply apply the `type='time'` attribute to a
[**Text Input**](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-textinput--docs) for
accepting time input.

<Canvas of={TextInputStories.TimePicker} />

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=7952-10519">
  Time Picker component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>
